did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a U-Net convolutional neural network (CNN) for automated lesion segmentation and a support-vector machine (SVM) classifier for detecting skin cancer malignancy using active infrared thermography features.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the development of a novel automatic detection system using active infrared thermography, the implementation of a U-Net CNN for segmentation, and the evaluation of a support-vector machine classifier. The paper reports specific results, such as a 17% improvement in the Jaccard index and comparative accuracy metrics against expert dermatologists and ResNet152V2, which are characteristic of original research rather than a review.

title: An automatic approach to detect skin cancer utilizing active infrared thermography
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic skin cancer detection system utilizing active infrared thermography to distinguish between malignant and benign lesions. The approach automates lesion segmentation using a U-Net convolutional neural network trained on the HAM10000 dataset and extracts novel spatial-temporal features for classification via a support-vector machine. The system was evaluated on a private clinical dataset (UdeC) containing low-quality images captured with a smartphone camera. Results showed that the U-Net segmentation achieved a Jaccard index of 0.60, performing comparably to non-expert dermatologist segmentation. The detection algorithm achieved an accuracy of 75.29% using U-Net segmentation, outperforming the original manual-selection algorithm and demonstrating a 3% higher accuracy than the ResNet152V2 network on low-quality clinical images.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in both HAM10000 (1113 images) and UdeC (7 images) datasets. Used in the classification task to distinguish malignant from benign lesions.
Included in both HAM10000 (514 images) and UdeC (41 images) datasets. Used in the classification task to distinguish malignant from benign lesions.
Included in UdeC dataset (9 images). Not present in the subset of HAM10000 used for training/validation (0 images). Used in the classification task to distinguish malignant from benign lesions.
Included in both HAM10000 (1700 images) and UdeC (87 images) datasets. Used as the comparator class to malignant lesions in the binary classification task.

all_task_types.target: Malignancy status (malignant vs. benign) of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The study uses a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to detect malignancy based on spatial-temporal features extracted from active infrared thermography. The task is binary classification distinguishing between malignant (MM, BCC, SCC) and benign lesions. The paper also mentions a U-Net CNN for lesion segmentation, but the primary diagnostic prediction task is classification.
A U-Net convolutional neural network is used to automatically segment the lesion area from the visible image to automate the lesion selection process, replacing manual selection.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images available on the Harvard Dataverse website, protected by a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial International Public License 4.0. It contains images of common skin cancer categories.

use.training: Used to train the U-Net convolutional neural network for lesion segmentation. 70% of the data was allocated for training and validation.
use.validation: Used for validating the U-Net segmentation algorithm. 70% of the data was allocated for training and validation combined.
use.testing: 30% of the data was used for testing the U-Net segmentation algorithm.

datasets_used.name: UdeC
datasets_used.description: A private dataset acquired by one of the authors during his doctoral research with support from the University of New Mexico Dermatology Clinic staff. It consists of low-quality, poorly illuminated images captured in clinical trials using a smartphone camera and active infrared thermography. The data includes image cubes with visible images and IR images corresponding to thermal recovery after cold excitation. Captured under protocol code 10-304 approved by the Human Research Review Committee of the University of New Mexico.

use.testing: Used as the final test set to evaluate the performance of the U-Net segmentation algorithm on visible images and to evaluate the detection algorithm's accuracy on low-quality clinical images.
use.other: Used to evaluate the detection algorithm's performance compared to expert dermatologist segmentation and other methods.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To model representative thermal recovery curves (TRCs) using a double exponential function and estimate initial temperatures.
To reduce the dimensionality of the difference vector of exponential model parameters (from five components to three principal components) for feature extraction.
To measure the similarity between modeled TRCs and arbitrary TRCs by computing covariance and standard deviations.
To aggregate projection, correlation, and distance features calculated over a set of at least 10 TRCs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and Jaccard index without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The U-Net segmentation algorithm was trained on 70% of this dataset and tested on the remaining 30% (987 samples). The dataset contained 3,327 images of Malignant Melanoma (1,113), Basal Cell Carcinoma (514), and Benign lesions (1,700).

Díaz Algorithm (Semi-automatic).Jaccard Index: Worst performance (value not explicitly stated, but lower than U-Net)

Proposed Automatic System (SVM with U-Ne.Comparison: 3% higher accuracy than ResNet152V2 on low-quality clinical images

UdeC.notes: The UdeC dataset contained 144 image cubes (7 MM, 41 BCC, 9 SCC, 87 BN). The U-Net segmentation performance showed a reduction in Jaccard index by 0.19 points and precision by 15% compared to HAM10000 results due to lower image quality and different mask criteria. The detection algorithm's accuracy of 75.29% was achieved using the U-Net segmentation.

architecture_used.main_model: U-Net convolutional neural network (CNN) variant proposed by Bisla et al.
architecture_used.input_size: 380x380 pixels (RGB image concatenated with HSV and L*a*b transformations, resulting in a 380x380x7 tensor)

architecture_details.backbone: U-Net variant with modifications including up-sample stages and dilation kernels of size 2x2 and 4x4
architecture_details.post_processing: Morphological dilation and erosion filters (scrubbing) and connected components analysis

training_details.data_augmentation: Vertical and horizontal flip (50% probability), brightness adjustments (±50%), saturation adjustments (±20%), contrast adjustments (±50%), and Gaussian blur (kernel size 5x5, std dev 0.1-20)
training_details.validation_samples: Included in the 70% training/validation split of HAM10000

preprocessing_techniques: Color normalization using the Shades of Gray algorithm to standardize color conditions affected by illumination and setup variations
Image resizing to 380x380 pixels to meet U-Net input requirements
Color space transformation: Concatenating RGB image with HSV and L*a*b transformations to create a 380x380x7 input tensor

Data augmentation during training: Vertical and horizontal flips (50% probability), brightness adjustments (±50%), saturation adjustments (±20%), contrast adjustments (±50%), and Gaussian blur (kernel size 5x5, standard deviation 0.1-20)
Post-segmentation morphological filtering (scrubbing): Application of dilation and erosion filters to smooth segmented areas and fill holes
Connected components analysis to select the largest segmentation area if multiple segments are detected
Inverse resizing of the output mask to match the original input image dimensions

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a 50% probability of being flipped.
Added using a Gaussian kernel filter of size 5x5 with a variable standard deviation randomly selected between 0.1 and 20.

conclusions: The proposed automatic detection system, which combines U-Net-based lesion segmentation with a support-vector machine (SVM) classifier using novel spatial-temporal features from active infrared thermography, achieves an accuracy of 75.29% on low-quality clinical images.
The automated system achieves 3% higher accuracy than the ResNet152V2 deep learning network when processing low-quality images obtained in a clinical setting.

The U-Net convolutional neural network automates lesion segmentation, achieving a Jaccard index of 0.60 on the private UdeC dataset, which represents a 17% improvement over the semi-automatic Díaz algorithm and performs comparably to non-expert dermatologist segmentation.

At an 85% sensitivity threshold, expert manual segmentation provided the highest specificity (87.62%), while non-expert and U-Net segmentations achieved comparable specificities of 69.63% and 68.80%, respectively, outperforming semi-automatic segmentation (64.45%).
The system addresses the limitation of manual lesion selection by providing a standardized and efficient automated solution that performs comparably to high-accuracy expert methods.

The performance of the segmentation algorithm decreased significantly (Jaccard index reduced by 0.19 points and precision by 15%) when applied to the UdeC dataset compared to the HAM10000 dataset due to lower image quality and different mask creation criteria.

The selection of the thermal recovery curve (TRC) area is crucial for detection performance, with expert knowledge yielding the best results, indicating that automated segmentation is a necessary compromise for efficiency when expert selection is not feasible.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, private clinical dataset (UdeC, n=144) with low-quality smartphone images, limiting generalizability and external validation.

The U-Net segmentation model was trained on high-quality dermoscopic images (HAM10000) and tested on low-quality clinical images, causing a significant performance drop (Jaccard index from 0.79 to 0.60) due to domain shift.
The UdeC dataset has a severe class imbalance, particularly for Malignant Melanoma (7 cases) and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (9 cases), which may bias the classifier.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons rely on descriptive metrics.

The system's accuracy (75.29%) is substantially lower than deep learning models on dermoscopy (often >90%), attributed to the difficulty of distinguishing benign from malignant lesions using active thermography on low-quality images.

The segmentation ground truth for the UdeC dataset was defined by an expert considering lesion nature rather than just pigmentation, creating a potential mismatch with the HAM10000 training masks which were based on pigmented areas.
The detection algorithm's performance is highly dependent on the accuracy of the lesion segmentation; incorrect segmentation leads to significant performance degradation.

The study addresses a binary classification problem (malignant vs. benign) including all cancer types, which is clinically more challenging than melanoma-only classification tasks common in literature.
